name: Elon Musk
evidenceSummary: Consistently stakes massive capital on low-probability/high-impact outcomes (SpaceX reusability, Tesla electrification). Exhibits high communication volatility via social media. Shows deep reluctance to delegate core engineering decisions, frequently overriding managers. Defies institutional norms while maintaining decades-long horizons on Mars colonization.

name: Tim Cook
evidenceSummary: Mastery of supply chain control and operational leverage. Exhibits extreme strategic patience, waiting for markets to mature rather than seeking first-mover novelty (AR, wearables). Highly institutional, minimizing communication volatility and relying heavily on a deeply trusted, highly delegated executive team.

name: Warren Buffett
evidenceSummary: Operates with generational time horizons. Refuses to invest in novel systems outside his competence circle, resulting in low novelty seeking and adaptability. Extreme delegation preference, famously allowing portfolio company CEOs total operational autonomy while centralizing only capital allocation.

name: Hyman Rickover
evidenceSummary: Built the Nuclear Navy through relentless, uncompromising control. Personally interviewed every officer candidate to ensure zero defect tolerance. Exhibited massive institutional orientation while simultaneously fighting military bureaucracy. Zero willingness to delegate safety or engineering standards.

name: Jimmy Donaldson
evidenceSummary: Relentless reinvestment of capital into production scale. Highly reliant on consensus data (audience retention metrics, CTR) to adapt rapidly. High execution bias with a willingness to scrap multi-million dollar projects if they fail internal pacing metrics. Builds high-control environments outside traditional media institutions.

name: Rick Rubin
evidenceSummary: Operates purely on intuition and reduction. Radically low control preference in the studio, preferring to create environments where artists self-correct. Zero institutional orientation. High conviction in personal taste, ignoring industry consensus on commercial viability to achieve breakthrough cultural resonance.

name: Lee Kuan Yew
evidenceSummary: Transformed a resource-poor island into a first-world nation through multi-decade strategic patience and rigorous institutional orientation. Low reliance on Western political consensus, highly pragmatic adaptability in economic policies, while maintaining strict personal control over domestic order.

name: Paul Graham
evidenceSummary: Institutionalized early-stage investing by creating a scalable filter for outliers (Y Combinator). High novelty seeking in finding founders. Low consensus reliance, explicitly writing essays that challenge prevailing tech dogma. High delegation preference, empowering founders rather than controlling them.

name: Jane Goodall
evidenceSummary: Revolutionized primatology by ignoring established institutional consensus (naming subjects instead of numbering them). Demonstrated extreme strategic patience, conducting localized field research for decades. Low communication volatility, maintaining a steady, diplomatic advocacy voice globally.

name: Reed Hastings
evidenceSummary: Famous for pivoting Netflix from DVDs to streaming, and then to original content, showing elite adaptability. Engineered a corporate culture based on radical candor and zero control preference ('Freedom and Responsibility'), exhibiting one of the highest delegation preferences in modern business.

name: Peter Thiel
evidenceSummary: Systematically searches for zero-to-one anomalies. Low institutional orientation, actively funding efforts to bypass traditional universities (Thiel Fellowship) and financial systems (crypto). Lowest possible consensus reliance, viewing agreement as a sign of intellectual failure.

name: Taylor Swift
evidenceSummary: Demonstrates absolute control preference over masters, touring logistics, and brand narrative. High strategic patience seen in the multi-year project to re-record her entire catalog to reclaim IP ownership. Adapts to new genres to maintain cultural dominance while holding strict institutional discipline in execution.

name: George S. Patton
evidenceSummary: Highest possible execution bias. Pioneered mobile armored warfare requiring rapid adaptation and high risk tolerance. Deeply struggled with strategic patience and institutional orientation, frequently clashing with Allied command due to high communication volatility and an inability to wait for logistical consensus.

name: Bob Iger
evidenceSummary: Master of institutional integration and M&A (Pixar, Marvel, Lucasfilm). High delegation preference, empowering acquired studios to retain creative autonomy. Very low communication volatility; operates purely through alignment, relationship preservation, and long-term brand stewardship.

name: Linus Torvalds
evidenceSummary: Maintains fierce architectural control over the Linux kernel. High communication volatility (historically blunt mailing list critiques). Prioritizes system stability and pragmatism over novelty. Built an anti-institutional open-source model but enforces strict meritocratic processes within it.

name: Gwynne Shotwell
evidenceSummary: Transforms visionary volatility into execution reality. Low communication volatility acts as a stabilizing force. High execution bias in scaling rapid launch cadences. Balances SpaceX's radical novelty with the institutional orientation required to win defense and NASA contracts.

name: John Boyd
evidenceSummary: Developed the OODA loop by aggressively fighting Pentagon procurement consensus. Zero institutional orientation, routinely sacrificing career advancement to maintain intellectual honesty. Maximum adaptability, formulating theories based on agility, rapid transients, and disrupting enemy cohesion.

name: Angela Merkel
evidenceSummary: Operated with extreme scientific caution and low risk tolerance. Highest levels of consensus reliance, often waiting for public opinion to solidify before moving. Near-zero communication volatility. Relied heavily on institutional frameworks (EU) to absorb and diffuse crises.

name: Brian Armstrong
evidenceSummary: Drives regulatory compliance in a fundamentally anti-institutional sector. Showed extreme conviction by publishing a 'no politics at work' mandate despite intense internal and external pushback. High strategic patience, focusing on building infrastructure through multi-year bear markets.

name: Sam Altman
evidenceSummary: Navigates complex geopolitical and capital environments to fund massive compute requirements. High novelty seeking combined with strategic long-term horizons. Shows high adaptability in restructuring OpenAI's non-profit/capped-profit model to absorb Microsoft capital while maintaining trajectory.

name: Jeff Bezos
evidenceSummary: Long-term investment in AWS and logistics infrastructure despite early losses; customer obsession driving experimentation. Pioneered two-pizza team delegation model while maintaining high control over product vision and long-term capital allocation.

name: Satya Nadella
evidenceSummary: Cultural shift at Microsoft toward cloud and empathy; iterative adaptation of legacy enterprise systems. Growth mindset transformation demonstrating high adaptability within institutional constraints.

name: Ray Dalio
evidenceSummary: Principles-based radical transparency and algorithmic decision-making at Bridgewater; economic cycle analysis. Built institutional frameworks to make decisions systematic and repeatable across macro regimes.

name: Mark Zuckerberg
evidenceSummary: Pivots from desktop to mobile, acquisitions (Instagram), metaverse long bet despite criticism. Maintains high voting control while executing decade-long platform bets against consensus.

name: James Mattis
evidenceSummary: Reading lists, deliberate decision-making, emphasis on professional military ethos. Combines institutional discipline with strategic autonomy; resigned on principle rather than compromise doctrine.

name: Nassim Taleb
evidenceSummary: Antifragile concepts, barbell strategies; public criticism of experts and fragility. Systematically exploits tail risks institutional models ignore, with high communication volatility used as epistemic provocation.

name: Indra Nooyi
evidenceSummary: Performance with Purpose at PepsiCo; long-term health/sustainability shifts against short-term shareholder pressure. Navigated institutional resistance through coalition-building and incremental cultural change.

name: Larry Page
evidenceSummary: Moonshot projects at Google/X; 10x thinking over incrementalism. Created Alphabet structure to preserve moonshot capacity while managing institutional scale demands.

name: Malcolm Gladwell
evidenceSummary: Pattern-seeking narratives across books; iterative storytelling refinement. Synthesizes counterintuitive research into mass-market ideas, operating at the intersection of academia and popular culture.

name: Winston Churchill
evidenceSummary: WWII defiance and alliance management; historical pattern recognition. Operated against consensus at critical junctures, using high communication volatility as a strategic instrument for public mobilization.

name: Sara Blakely
evidenceSummary: Bootstrapped Spanx with iterative product feedback and resilience to rejection. Maintained full ownership and control through unconventional market entry, defying institutional retail gatekeepers.

name: Andrew Ng
evidenceSummary: AI education scaling (Coursera) and iterative deep learning advancements. Combines academic rigor with mass-market accessibility; operates at the boundary of institutional research and entrepreneurial execution.

name: Condoleezza Rice
evidenceSummary: Foreign policy realism and institutional navigation. Operates within institutional power structures with precision, using consensus reliance as a tool for coalition building across adversarial geopolitical environments.

name: Vitalik Buterin
evidenceSummary: Ethereum evolution through upgrades and community governance. Maintains high-novelty intellectual output while navigating decentralized consensus mechanisms that reject traditional control structures.

name: Katherine Graham
evidenceSummary: Washington Post during Pentagon Papers/Watergate: institutional defense under existential pressure. Overcame self-doubt to make high-conviction decisions that protected press freedom against state power.
